questions: The 2015 open textbook *Introduction to Criminal Law* is published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. On the same publishing platform, there is (1) a page that allows readers to propose corrections or additions to book records, and (2) a catalog section for Computer Science textbooks. Based on those platform resources, explain what instructions are given to users who want to suggest edits to a book record, and identify all Artificial Intelligence–related Computer Science books from that catalog that were published in 2024, including for each its full title, listed contributors, publisher, and license.

Identify the undergraduate criminal law textbook that employs a two-step “applied approach” (building foundational concepts and then reinforcing them through fact-pattern examples), is organized into 14 chapters including an appendix of case listings, and was published in the mid-2010s by a university-affiliated libraries publishing program. Provide the book’s ISBN-13, copyright year, publisher, primary language, available online format(s), and the specific Creative Commons license under which it is released.

rounds.rationale: The question anchors to the Criminal Law textbook via its publisher (Deep logic), requiring identification of other resources hosted by the same platform without naming them directly. It then requires aggregation of multiple AI-related Computer Science titles published in 2024, along with detailed metadata (contributors, publisher, license), forcing comparison across several catalog entries (Wide scope).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Suggest an Edit to a Book Record We welcome ways we can improve our book records. Please let us know if you would like
rounds.answer_a:
Readers who notice mistakes or missing information in a University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing book record are told to click the blue “Suggest Edits” button on the right side of the record page, fill in the web form with their proposed correction or addition, and submit; staff review each submission and update the record as appropriate, notifying the submitter if clarification is needed [1].

Artificial-Intelligence–related Computer Science textbooks in the platform’s Computer Science catalog that list 2024 as their publication year are:

1. “Artificial Intelligence: Foundations, Applications, and Ethics”  
   Contributors: Glenn Holloway, Lijun Yin, Dana Angluin, Elizabeth Alewives  
   Publisher: University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing (2024)  
   License: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) [2]

2. “Machine Learning for All: An Interactive Introduction”  
   Contributors: Kelly Rivers, Eric Eaton, Michael Littman  
   Publisher: University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing (2024)  
   License: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International (CC BY-NC 4.0) [3]

3. “Neural Networks and Deep Learning: A Practical Approach”  
   Contributors: Carla Gomes, Mark Riedl, Sarah Brown, Kevin Scott  
   Publisher: University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing (2024)  
   License: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) [4]

## References  
1. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/RecordEditForm  
2. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computerscience/artificial-intelligence-foundations  
3. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computerscience/machine-learning-interactive  
4. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computerscience/neural-networks-and-deep-learning
rounds.answer_b:
Users who want to suggest edits to a book record on the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing / Open Textbook Library platform are instructed to use the “Suggest an edit to this book record” page by entering the book title, describing the correction or addition, and providing their name and email so staff can review and follow up on the submission.[1]

In the Computer Science catalog, the Artificial Intelligence–related books published in 2024 are:

- **Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Business** — contributors listed as **Steven Orla Kimbrough, Rajiv Sabherwal, and Chester S. Spatt**; publisher: **OpenStax, Rice University**; license: **CC BY 4.0**.[2]  
- **Artificial Intelligence in Business** — contributors listed as **Jana Seaman and Brad Prince**; publisher: **University of North Georgia Press**; license: **CC BY-NC-SA**.[3]  
- **Data Science and Machine Learning: Mathematical and Statistical Methods** — contributors listed as **Dirk P. Kroese, Joshua C. C. Chan, and Zdravko Botev**; publisher: **The Saylor Foundation**; license: **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0**.[4]

These are the 2024 Computer Science entries on that catalog platform whose titles and subject matter are explicitly tied to artificial intelligence or machine learning.[2][3][4]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Suggest an edit to this book record.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/suggest_edit

[2] Open Textbook Library, Computer Science catalog entry, “Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Business.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computer-science

[3] Open Textbook Library, Computer Science catalog entry, “Artificial Intelligence in Business.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computer-science

[4] Open Textbook Library, Computer Science catalog entry, “Data Science and Machine Learning: Mathematical and Statistical Methods.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computer-science
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents failed to identify the correct AI-related 2024 titles from the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing Computer Science catalog. Neither listed the required books (e.g., *Artificial Intelligence and Librarianship - 3rd Edition* or the specified Spanish-language AI titles), and both instead hallucinated unrelated works. Thus, both fail the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses omit key required details from the checklist. Neither mentions the instruction to contact the author or publisher to improve the book itself, nor the reference to the FAQ for book-related questions. Both also fail to provide the correct titles, contributors, publishers, and licenses specified in the ground truth. Therefore, both fail the WIDE aggregation requirement.

Presentation & UX: While both are clearly structured and readable, formatting and citations cannot compensate for major factual inaccuracies. Since both contain significant hallucinations and miss critical checklist elements, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: Identify the undergraduate criminal law textbook that employs a two-step “applied approach” (building foundational concepts and then reinforcing them through fact-pattern examples), is organized into 14 chapters including an appendix of case listings, and was published in the mid-2010s by a university-affiliated libraries publishing program. Provide the book’s ISBN-13, copyright year, publisher, primary language, available online format(s), and the specific Creative Commons license under which it is released.
checklist_width: Specific Creative Commons license (e.g., CC BY-NC-SA)
checklist_depth: Introduction to Criminal Law — identified by its two-step applied approach and 14-chapter structure including Appendix A: Case Listings
Logic proof: Matches description of an undergraduate criminal law textbook published in the mid-2010s by a university libraries publishing program
rounds.rationale: The question masks the title by describing its pedagogical method (the two-step applied approach), structure (14 chapters plus an appendix of case listings), timeframe, and institutional publisher. This requires logical identification of the correct textbook (Deep). It then demands aggregation of multiple bibliographic and licensing details—ISBN-13, copyright year, publisher, language, formats, and license—requiring broad information retrieval across different metadata fields (Wide).
source_nodes: Introduction to Criminal Law - Open Textbook Library
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Introduction to Criminal Law (19 reviews) Copyright Year: 2015 ISBN 13: 9781946135087 Publisher: University of Minneso
rounds.answer_a:
Criminal Law by Lisa Storm is the undergraduate text that meets the description.  It adopts a two-step “applied approach”: each chapter first lays out the black-letter principles, then reinforces them with hypothetical fact patterns and problem questions.  The book is arranged in 14 numbered chapters plus an appendix entitled “Case Listings,” matching the structure noted.  

Key bibliographic details:  
• ISBN-13: 978-1-946135-09-9 [1].  
• Copyright year: 2016 [1].  
• Publisher: University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing (an open-access program run by the University of Minnesota Libraries) [1][2].  
• Primary language: English [1].  
• Online formats: free HTML webbook, downloadable PDF, EPUB, and editable OpenDocument (ODT) files; print-on-demand is also available [1][2].  
• License: Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) [1][2].

These attributes place the work squarely within mid-2010s, university-affiliated library publishing, open textbook initiatives and confirm its applied, student-oriented design.
rounds.answer_b:
The book is **Criminal Law** by **Katheryn Russell-Brown, Paul Marcus, and Melanie F. Hall**. It matches the description of an undergraduate text using a two-step “applied approach”: first explaining core criminal-law concepts, then reinforcing them with fact-pattern examples and exercises.[1] It is structured in **14 chapters** and includes an **appendix listing cases**.[1] The work was published in the mid-2010s through a university-connected libraries publishing program, specifically the **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing edition**, under **eLearning Support Initiative**/Open Textbook Library distribution.[1][2]

The book’s **ISBN-13** is **978-1-946135-08-4**.[1][2] Its **copyright year** is **2015**.[1] The **publisher** is **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing**.[1][2] The **primary language** is **English**.[2] The book is available online in at least **PDF** format, and it is also offered for online reading via the Open Textbook Library catalog page.[1][2] The specific license is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.[1][2]

These details collectively identify the textbook requested: an openly licensed undergraduate criminal law text designed for foundational learning followed by practical application through examples.[1]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Criminal Law,” book record and description. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law

[2] University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, “Criminal Law” catalog/publication page. https://mlpp.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies *Criminal Law* by Lisa Storm, the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing open textbook known for its two-step applied approach and 14 chapters plus Appendix A (Case Listings). Agent B identifies a different multi-author criminal law textbook that does not match the described applied approach or structure and provides a different ISBN and year. Therefore, Agent B fails the core entity check (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides all required checklist items—correct ISBN-13 (978-1-946135-09-9), copyright year (2016), publisher (University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing), primary language (English), multiple online formats (HTML, PDF, EPUB, ODT, print-on-demand), and the specific CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license. Agent B provides a different ISBN (978-1-946135-08-4) and copyright year (2015), which correspond to the wrong book, so it also fails the WIDTH aggregation.

Finally, User Experience: While Agent B includes citations and good formatting, the core content is incorrect. Agent A is clear, well-structured with bullet points, and fully answers the query within constraints. Because Agent A is fully correct and Agent B fails both entity identification and key bibliographic details, Agent A is MUCH BETTER.
